summary: I was born in St. Petersburg, where my early musical education began. My aunt, Isabelle Vengerova, gave me my first piano lessons there. During World War I, I served in a regimental band in St. Petersburg before the revolution forced me to leave the city.

My aunt, Isabelle Vengerova, was an accomplished pianist who first taught me piano. She discovered that I had perfect pitch early on, which shaped my musicianship. Later, when she became a professor at the St. Petersburg Conservatory, I studied in her piano class.

I entered the piano class at the St. Petersburg Conservatory under my aunt Isabelle Vengerova. Before admission, I auditioned before Alexander Glazunov and received the highest grade. This institution was central to my early formal musical training.

Alexander Glazunov personally auditioned me for admission to the St. Petersburg Conservatory, and I was honored to receive the highest possible grade from him. This was a pivotal moment early in my musical education.

When World War I broke out, I was called up for service but played in a regimental band in St. Petersburg rather than fighting on the front. The war and subsequent revolution made life dangerous, prompting me to leave Petrograd and move south.

After leaving Petrograd due to the revolution, I spent time displaced in several places, including Kiev between 1918 and 1923. This period was marked by uncertainty and movement across various cities.
During my years of displacement from 1918 to 1923, I lived in Yalta among other places. This was part of my journey southward after leaving Petrograd amid the revolution.
Istanbul was one of the cities where I stayed between 1918 and 1923 while displaced by the upheavals in Russia. It was a significant stop before I eventually settled in Paris.
Between 1918 and 1923, I spent some time in Bulgaria as part of my displacement from Russia. This period was marked by frequent moves before I finally reached Paris.

I arrived in Paris after years of displacement and met Serge Koussevitzky there, who hired me as a rehearsal pianist. This connection was crucial for my exposure to modern music, including Stravinsky's works. Paris was a turning point before I moved to the United States in 1923.

Serge Koussevitzky engaged me as a rehearsal pianist in Paris, which helped me learn the works of Stravinsky and other modern composers. I even prepared a performing edition of Le Sacre du Printemps that he used for many years. Later, I worked with him again in Boston, though our relationship eventually soured.

I prepared a performing edition of Le Sacre du Printemps for Serge Koussevitzky, which he used extensively in his concerts. This work was central to my early involvement with modern music during my time in Paris.

I arrived in the United States in 1923 to work as an opera coach at the Eastman School of Music in Rochester. Though I initially spoke no English, I learned quickly with help from Paul Horgan. Later, I worked in Boston and became involved with American modern composers.
My first position in the United States was as an opera coach at the Eastman School of Music in Rochester starting in 1923. This role marked the beginning of my American career in music education.
Rochester was where I began my American career at the Eastman School of Music in 1923. It was a challenging time as I adapted to a new language and culture with the help of Paul Horgan.
Paul Horgan was a friend who helped me learn English when I first arrived in the United States. His support was invaluable during my early years at the Eastman School of Music in Rochester.

In Boston, I resumed working with Serge Koussevitzky before our relationship ended. I also founded the Chamber Orchestra of Boston in 1927 to promote avant-garde American composers like Henry Cowell and Charles Ives.

I organized the Chamber Orchestra of Boston in 1927 with the goal of premiering works by avant-garde American composers such as Henry Cowell and Charles Ives. We gave important premieres including Ives's Three Places in New England and Cowell's Suite for piano and orchestra.

I was committed to presenting the challenging music of Henry Cowell, conducting the premiere of his Suite for piano and orchestra with the Chamber Orchestra of Boston. His avant-garde style was part of my focus on modern American composers.
cues: Suite for piano and orchestra premiere

summary: I conducted the premiere of Charles Ives's Three Places in New England with the Chamber Orchestra of Boston. Ives's music was difficult for many conductors, but I was dedicated to bringing it to audiences despite resistance.
cues: Three Places in New England premiere

summary: I had the honor of conducting the premiere of Charles Ives's Three Places in New England with the Chamber Orchestra of Boston. This work was part of my efforts to promote challenging modern American compositions.

I premiered Henry Cowell's Suite for piano and orchestra with the Chamber Orchestra of Boston, showcasing my commitment to avant-garde American music. This was part of my broader mission to present new and difficult works.

In 1934, I programmed Arnold Schoenberg's music alongside Charles Ives's at the Hollywood Bowl, but the audience reacted very negatively. This event marked the abrupt end of my conducting career due to perceptions that I was too radical.

At the Hollywood Bowl in 1934, I conducted a concert featuring works by Charles Ives and Arnold Schoenberg. The audience's negative reaction to this program effectively ended my conducting career because I was seen as too radical.
After my conducting career ended, I turned to musicology and wrote important books like Music Since 1900. This work reflects my deep engagement with modern music history and scholarship.

I authored the Lexicon of Musical Invective, a book that explores the harsh criticism faced by modern music. This was part of my broader work in musicology and lexicography after I stopped conducting.

I traveled extensively to South America to promote modern music and foster cultural exchange. These trips were part of my efforts to support contemporary composers and broaden musical horizons internationally.

I also traveled to the Soviet Union to promote modern music and encourage cultural exchange. This was significant given my Russian origins and the political changes that had displaced me years earlier.

entity: Thesaurus of Scales and Melodic Patterns
summary: In 1948, I published the Thesaurus of Scales and Melodic Patterns, which became very popular among jazz musicians. This work represents my contribution to music theory and education, bridging classical and jazz traditions.

entity: International Cyclopedia of Music and Musicians
summary: I have edited major reference works including the International Cyclopedia of Music and Musicians. This editorial work reflects my dedication to providing comprehensive resources for musicians and scholars.

I also edited Baker's Biographical Dictionary of Musicians, helping to document the lives and careers of significant figures in music. This was part of my broader work in music lexicography and scholarship.

My first published work in the United States was a piano suite called Studies in Black and White, released in 1928. This composition marked my early efforts as a composer in America alongside my other musical activities.
